title: Spinning from lock to yarn
subtitle: Processing an individual fleece lock without carding or combing
excerpt: Lock spinning bypasses both carding and combing: the spinner opens each lock tip-first, drafts from the cut end, and draws out the fibres as they naturally lie. This preserves the crimp structure and produces a lustrous, semi-worsted yarn with a clear fibre direction. Longwool breeds such as Wensleydale, Teeswater, and Gotland give the most satisfying results.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from published fleece-to-yarn guides and UK spinning guild teaching materials. Lock spinning (spinning directly from the lock without carding or combing) is well documented for longwool breeds in handspinning reference books. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A single natural cluster of fleece fibres that grows together and is separated from the fleece when shorn. Each lock has a tip (the weather-exposed end) and a cut end (where the shears cut).
Seeds, hay, straw, or other plant debris caught in the raw fleece. Usually abbreviated to VM. Some VM falls out during processing; stubborn pieces must be picked out by hand.
A yarn that has some fibre alignment but not the full parallel order of a combed worsted preparation. Lock spun yarn sits in this category.
The length of a single lock of fleece measured from tip to cut end. It determines which prep method and drafting style will work best.
Abbreviation for vegetable matter: seeds, hay, straw, and other plant debris found in raw fleece.

content.text: Lock spinning is one of the simplest routes in handspinning. Wash the fleece, open each

tip-first to free any felted tip, draft from the cut end, and spin. No carding, no combing. It works because longwool breeds grow their fibres in an aligned formation within each lock. Open the lock correctly and draft in the direction of the fibre length, and the fibres draw out smoothly without any mechanical prep.

in character: smooth and lustrous, with a clear fibre direction and the breed's natural wave or curl. Wensleydale, Teeswater, and Gotland are the best breeds for this method. Their long, lustrous locks (often 15-25 cm
) spin well directly from the lock.
Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair and roll up sleeves before sitting at the wheel.

Lock spinning works best with breeds whose fibres grow in clearly defined, separable locks with a staple of at least 12 cm. Wensleydale, Teeswater, and Gotland are ideal. Bluefaced Leicester and Lincoln Longwool also work well. Fine Merino is more difficult: its very short staple and tight crimp make the fibres inclined to draft unevenly when spun from the lock without preparation, and the tip weathering on Merino locks is often more significant.
Pick through the washed locks before spinning and remove any

in a lock-spun yarn is more visible than in a carded yarn because the fibres are not thoroughly mixed. Hold each lock up to the light before opening it: small seeds appear as dark spots. Tease them out with your fingertips or use a flicker brush (also called a wool flicker) to open the tip end and dislodge seeds. Heavily VM-loaded locks are worth discarding rather than spending time picking.

Hold the lock by the cut end in your back hand, with the tip pointing toward the orifice. Open the tip slightly with your front hand to separate the fibres, then pinch ahead of the twist and draw the back hand away from the orifice in a long, smooth draft. The fibres should draw out in the direction they grew, from cut end to tip.

Use a moderate ratio: too high a ratio and the twist races up the lock before you can draft; too low and the yarn will be thick and under-twisted. A 6:1 to 8:1 starting ratio works for most medium-longwool locks. Keep your treadle speed steady and let the lock feed at the rate the fibres want to draft, which is slower than carded preparation because the lock is longer.

When one lock runs out, overlap the tip end of the spent lock with the cut end of a new one by 3-4 cm and hold them together briefly as you draft. The twist will catch the new lock fibres and draw them in. Keep the join in the drafting zone rather than letting it advance into the already-twisted yarn before the fibres are fully integrated.

items.problem: The lock drafts unevenly: thick sections near the cut end and thin at the tip.
items.solution: The cut end of many longwool locks is denser than the tip, so a small amount of variation is normal. Open the cut end slightly with a flicker before starting to draft, which loosens the fibre distribution at the base.

items.problem: The twist races up the lock and locks it before I can draft.
items.solution: Lower the drive ratio and slow your treadle speed. You need more time between each treadle revolution for a long lock than for a carded preparation. You can also apply a light back tension to the lock with your back hand, slowing the twist advance.

items.problem: Joins between locks are visible as slubs in the finished yarn.
items.solution: The overlap at the join was too short or too far advanced into the twisted section. Practice joining with the overlap held entirely in the drafting zone, and let the twist draw the fibres together naturally before the join moves toward the orifice.
